title: Fei-Fei Li sparked an AI boom — now she won't let humans fall behind

# Fei-Fei Li sparked an AI boom — now she won't let humans fall behind

## As the cofounder World Labs, she just raised $1B in funding to expand AI into “spatial intelligence.” She wants to keep humans at the core every step of the way

Wendy Naugle

USA TODAY

March 2, 2026 Updated March 30, 2026, 7:30 a.m. ET

Hear this story

[](https://www.facebook.com/dialog/share?display=popup&app_id=1279862243338849&href=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.usatoday.com%2Fstory%2Ftech%2F2026%2F03%2F02%2Ffei-fei-li-women-of-the-year-2026%2F88655019007%2F) [](https://x.com/intent/post?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.usatoday.com%2Fstory%2Ftech%2F2026%2F03%2F02%2Ffei-fei-li-women-of-the-year-2026%2F88655019007%2F&text=Fei-Fei%20Li%20sparked%20an%20AI%20boom%20%E2%80%94%20now%20she%20won't%20let%20humans%20fall%20behind&via=usatoday) 

_Fei-Fei Li is one of USA TODAY’s 2026 Women of the Year, a recognition of women who have made a significant impact in their communities and beyond. Meet this year's honorees_ _here_ _._

On Feb. 18, Fei-Fei Li made headlines. The company she cofounded, World Labs, announced they’d raised $1 billion ‒ with a B ‒ in funding. Now they're poised to bring artificial intelligence into 3D. That’s the “spatial intelligence” that will make it possible for robots to do the dishes or sort your socks.

Even Li couldn’t imagine that would be possible 20 years ago, when she was fresh off her PhD and a young assistant professor working in AI. At the time, the field was the stuff of sci-fi dystopian movies like "The Matrix" or "I, Robot" to the public. In the research community, the technology showed promise but had largely stalled.

“I entered AI smack in the middle of AI winter,” she says.

But Li had a theory. Humans learn visually; we absorb vast amounts of information by looking at the world around us. AI, she believed, needed to learn the way humans learn. Not just with algorithms, but pictures.

USA TODAY Shopping: [Shop sales in tech, home, fashion, beauty & more curated by our editors.](https://www.usatoday.com/shopping/)

“I knew it was a contrarian idea,” she says, “but I felt strongly that this is one of the absolute missing pieces.”

Li's hypothesis got little notice: She coauthored a small paper and presented it at a conference without much fanfare. But she made the data set publicly available and issued a challenge. Think of it like an Olympics of AI: The ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge in 2010 gathered the best in the world to compete to teach a computer to "see” what’s in a photo, using the same massive trove of images and the same rules.

It was, by design, democratic: open, transparent, inclusive. Competitive. Human. “Science is about openness, it’s about debate, it's about letting the ideas flow,” Li, 49, says. “We test and experiment and debate each other's ideas.” ImageNet quickly became one of the driving forces of AI by providing the data the field had been hungry for and by establishing a benchmark for progress.

## AI and cucumbers (yes, cucumbers)

Li began to grasp how transformational AI could be in 2017, when she took a sabbatical to work at Google. The size and scope of the tech giant were eye-opening. “My whole career up until that point, AI was a personal curiosity,” she says. As chief scientist of AI and machine learning, she worked with sectors ranging from health care to agriculture, manufacturing to energy, and quickly saw how AI would change everyone’s life . “Every business I worked with would be or was about to be changed by AI,” she says.

And every person: She learned about a Japanese farmer using Google’s AI technology. “He was helping his mom on her cucumber farm. And there's a very cumbersome step of sorting cucumbers,” she recalls. “He's a developer, he's a coder. So he used software from Google to automate that part of the process to help empower his mom. Here I was in Silicon Valley, working at the biggest company, coming from one of the best universities in the world ‒ and on the other side of the world, one person could use AI to change a small business.”

That kind of civilizational impact, she recognized, would require some ground rules. “It became a mission for me," she says. “We need lighthouses to guide where this technology is going. It cannot be for one company makingmoney. It has to be centered around human dignity, human well-being.”

She held on to that belief when Google faced backlash around Project Maven, a controversial contract with the Department of Defense that would use AI to analyze drone footage in war zones. Thousands of employees petitioned the company to stay out of “the business of war,” and some of Li’s emails were publicly leaked. She didn’t resign; instead, she helped author Google’s “AI Principles” for the responsible development of the technology. “I stayed because of my responsibility,” she says. “Every university, every company was grappling with how do we establish an industry norm? How do we establish a culture in each of our organizations to deal with what's to come?”

Once those guidelines were released to the public, she returned to Stanford with a sense of urgency: If AI were to shape humanity, then humanity needed to be at the very center of technological progress. She corralled colleagues across disciplines ‒ computer science, law, medicine, business, humanities ‒ and cofounded the Stanford Human‑Centered AI Institute in 2019, with the goal to study, research and most of all guide the human and societal impact of AI. She's using that expertise to help guide public policy. When California Governor Gavin Newsom wanted a new bill to [establish guardrails](https://www.gov.ca.gov/2024/09/29/governor-newsom-announces-new-initiatives-to-advance-safe-and-responsible-ai-protect-californians/) around AI, he tapped Li.

## The 'Godmother of AI'

Early in 2025, AI accounted for 92% of GDP growth, according to Harvard economist Jason Furman, and experts believe we are only at the beginning of the massive cultural change that machine learning will bring.

Li often says that whoever builds AI will influence what it becomes. She reminds her students not to be boxed in by labels, gender, role, or expectations. It's why she cofounded AI4ALL, which has helped thousands of students from underrepresented backgrounds learn AI at no cost.  She’s aware of both the promises and threats AI can bring. “Technology is always a double-edged sword,” she says. “Human civilization is a history of innovation. From the day we discovered fire, from the day we discovered you can grind a stone into an ax, from the invention of wheels, electricity, cars, airplanes, biotech, and now AI. Every step of the way, we intend to innovate for good, but we always have an underbelly as a species, to potentially use it for harm.” It’s why she feels Stanford Human‑Centered AI Institute is so critical. “ We need thought leadership to help us grapple with these really tough questions, and we need an ecosystem to work together to solve these hardest problems,” she says.

She believes her work can tip the scales toward AI’s potential for good. And there’s so much potential. “Many of us, in our daily life, are benefiting from AI,” she says. “I now literally, if I have a kid who is sick, not only do I talk to doctors and nurses, I can have a meaningful conversation with AI about how I can care for the kid.”

Her home is multigenerational, with her parents, who immigrated with her from China, living with her husband and their two teenagers. Her parents’ long‑term illnesses have shaped her interest in AI for health care and elder care. At the dinner table, conversations with her kids about AI focus on how it is both a responsibility and a tool: something to learn, to question, and to use for good.

Asked what she hopes her children will say when they look back on this moment in history, she doesn’t mention World Labs, Stanford Human‑Centered AI Institute, any of her awards, or 400-plus scientific papers. “I hope they’ll say I was a good mom,” she says.

It doesn’t get more human than that.

_Wendy Naugle_ _is USA Today's Executive Editor of Entertainment. Follow her on Instagram @wendy\_naugle._
